texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Problem: VOC recovery from an industrial gaseous stream
texts.text: Problem: VOC recovery from an industrial gaseous stream

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A membrane process is investigated for the recovery of an organic molecule (VOC) from a pressurised gaseous effluent. The inlet VOC concentration is 10 %vol in N 2 , the total gas flowrate is 1000 Nm 3 .h -1 , the inlet pressure is 20 atm and the permeate pressure 1.1 atm. For sake of simplicity, the module is supposed to be perfectly mixed. A commercially available membrane is proposed to achieve that purpose, with a 15 GPU VOC permeance and 0.48 GPU N2 permeance.
texts.text: A membrane process is investigated for the recovery of an organic molecule (VOC) from a pressurised gaseous effluent. The inlet VOC concentration is 10 %vol in N 2 , the total gas flowrate is 1000 Nm 3 .h -1 , the inlet pressure is 20 atm and the permeate pressure 1.1 atm. For sake of simplicity, the module is supposed to be perfectly mixed. A commercially available membrane is proposed to achieve that purpose, with a 15 GPU VOC permeance and 0.48 GPU N2 permeance.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A membrane gas separation module is initially pumped under vacuum. The high pressure (retentate) compartment is then quickly fed by air at a given pressure (valve 1 open) and the system is further let completely closed (valves 1, 2 and 3 closed). Oxygen is faster than nitrogen under permeation condition (i.e. pressure difference between retentate and permeate).
texts.text: A membrane gas separation module is initially pumped under vacuum. The high pressure (retentate) compartment is then quickly fed by air at a given pressure (valve 1 open) and the system is further let completely closed (valves 1, 2 and 3 closed). Oxygen is faster than nitrogen under permeation condition (i.e. pressure difference between retentate and permeate).
